additional_attendees.name: David Roosa
additional_attendees.title: VP of Sales (building out sales team)

next_steps: Schedule follow-up demo showing bundling scenario in Quotivity (Friday 11:00 AM ET)
Quotivity team to present how admin, network, and marketplace bundles would look inside the tool
Prospect may share go-to-market document and ops playbook when finalized

jtbd.job: Build quotes fast enough to keep pace with a growing sales team without operations bottlenecking every deal
jtbd.context: Operations lead and CFO currently QA every quote manually. Sales VP needs to get quotes out the door without waiting for operations to build or approve each one.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Sales reps self-serve quotes from pre-configured bundles and price books with guardrails, removing operations and finance from the critical path on standard deals

jtbd.job: Maintain a single, accurate price book across multiple solution partners with year-over-year rate changes
jtbd.context: Price book lives in SharePoint/Excel with hundreds of line items across multiple partner tabs. Rate books change annually (some mid-year). Risk of quoting from the wrong year's rates.
jtbd.desired_outcome: One system of record for pricing that automatically applies the correct rate book based on effective date and prevents quoting from stale rates

jtbd.job: Generate professional, customer-facing proposals that include cover letters, product descriptions, and signature pages directly from HubSpot
jtbd.context: Current HubSpot native quoting produces invoice-style output with static text that reps cannot edit. They want cover letters, network requirement disclosures, and letters of intent with e-signature.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Branded, editable proposal documents generated from the quote data in HubSpot with all required sections included

jtbd.job: Scale quoting from new business into renewals using the same system
jtbd.context: Account management currently handles renewals outside the quoting system. Want 120-day-out notifications and the ability to review, adjust, and release renewals through the same tool.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Unified quoting system for new business and renewals with automated renewal reminders and markup review workflows

pains.pain: Manual quoting process requires operations lead and CFO to QA every quote
pains.trigger: Every new quote. The operations lead builds or reviews each quote alongside the CFO before it can go out. This is the stated bottleneck they want to eliminate.
pains.cost: Operations leader's time consumed by quote QA instead of strategic work. Sales velocity limited by operations availability. Direct quote: 'I need to get out of doing these quotes with our CFO.'
pains.urgency_reason: Sales team is actively being built out. Current process does not scale beyond the current volume.

pains.pain: Price book sprawl across multiple Excel tabs with duplicated rows for year-over-year rate changes
pains.trigger: Solution partners send updated rate schedules annually (some mid-year). Each partner requires its own tab. Rate books for 2025, 2026, and 2027 coexist, creating confusion about which rates to use.
pains.cost: Risk of quoting wrong year's rates. Hundreds of line items condensed to 52 but still duplicated across years. Quote: 'What if I accidentally do like Cigna's rate for 26, but it's a 27 quote?'
pains.urgency_reason: Active quoting is happening now with multiple rate years in play. Errors have immediate revenue impact.

pains.pain: HubSpot native quoting cannot handle their pricing model (PEPM, percentage of savings, tiered by employee count, partner-specific rules)
pains.trigger: Attempted to configure price book in HubSpot. Could not capture per-employee-per-month vs. percentage-of-savings pricing mechanisms natively. Created extra rows as workarounds.
pains.cost: Bloated product catalog with unnecessary rows. Manual workarounds that break when new scenarios arise. Operations lead spent significant time trying to make native quoting work.
pains.urgency_reason: They are mid-implementation of 'HubSpot 2.0' and have identified quoting as their biggest gap.

pains.pain: No way to enforce partner-specific bundling rules (e.g., Cigna requires utilization management, claim editing, and P4P with every network quote)
pains.trigger: Each solution partner has mandatory products that must be included when their network is selected. Currently enforced through tribal knowledge and manual QA.
pains.cost: Risk of sending quotes that violate partner contract requirements. Manual enforcement does not scale as the sales team grows.
pains.urgency_reason: Currently mitigated by operations QA, but that QA is the bottleneck they want to remove.

pains.pain: Sales reps cannot edit quote documents (cover letters, product descriptions) in native HubSpot quoting
pains.trigger: Reps need to personalize cover letters and adjust product descriptions per prospect. Native HubSpot output is static, invoice-style format.
pains.cost: Reps cannot self-serve personalized proposals. Every customization requires operations involvement or workarounds outside HubSpot.
pains.urgency_reason: Directly impacts the sales team's ability to operate independently.

pains.pain: Need internal vs. external quote views with different levels of detail (margin data internal only, gross pricing external)
pains.trigger: Finance needs to see cost breakdown (partner cost, Reflect Health margin) but customers should only see the gross price. Currently managed through separate Excel versions.
pains.cost: Dual-maintenance of internal and external quote versions. Risk of sending the wrong version to a customer.
pains.urgency_reason: Every quote requires two versions today.

pains.pain: Discount approval process is informal and not systematically enforced
pains.trigger: Sales VP wants to offer competitive pricing. Discounts below standard rates require finance approval, but there is no automated escalation. Done through ad hoc communication.
pains.cost: Deals delayed waiting for informal approval. Risk of unauthorized discounts. No audit trail of discount decisions.
pains.urgency_reason: Sales VP is actively requesting discounts to win deals. Volume will increase as the team grows.

pains.pain: No date-driven logic to automatically apply the correct rate year based on quote effective date
pains.trigger: Sales rep enters a 1/1/27 quote but might accidentally pull 2026 rates because both exist side by side in the price book.
pains.cost: Incorrect pricing on quotes. Manual verification required for every quote to confirm correct rate year.
pains.urgency_reason: Multiple active rate years in the price book now (25, 26, 27 for some partners).

workflow_reality.current_process: Quote requests flow from the VP of Sales to the operations lead, who builds quotes in Excel templates (SharePoint-hosted). The operations lead and CFO review every quote before it goes out. Templates exist for three verticals (admin, network, marketplace). For repeat scenarios, the rep asks operations to clone an existing template and modify it. Finalized quotes are exported as branded documents with cover letter, pricing grid, service descriptions, and signature page.
tools_in_use: HubSpot (CRM, product catalog loaded but limited by native quoting capabilities)
SharePoint (price book storage and version control)
Excel (price book management, quote template construction, all calculations)
Word/PDF (final proposal document generation, manual formatting)
workflow_reality.team_size_quoting: 2 (operations lead + CFO for QA). VP of Sales is the primary quote requester. Building out a sales team with at least one additional rep (Trey) joining.
brittleness_indicators: Operations lead personally involved in every quote — single point of failure
CFO required for QA on every quote — finance bottleneck on sales velocity
Price book has hundreds of codes condensed to 52 but still spread across multiple tabs per partner
Rate year confusion: 25, 26, and 27 rates coexist with no logic to prevent cross-year errors
First scenario encountered in nearly a year that had no existing template — process breaks on novel deal structures
HubSpot native quoting described as 'just like an invoice' with static text — unusable for their proposal requirements
Partner-specific rules (Cigna mandatory products) enforced through tribal knowledge only

evaluated.name: HubSpot Native CPQ
evaluated.what_they_liked: Already in place. Product catalog loaded. Part of HubSpot ecosystem they are investing in.
evaluated.why_its_failing: Cannot handle PEPM vs. percentage-of-savings pricing mechanisms. No way to create editable proposal documents with cover letters. No partner-specific bundling rules. No tiered pricing by employee count without duplicating SKUs. Output is invoice-style, not proposal-style.
evaluated.key_quote: In the current configuration, cannot do this. David cannot edit this. None of this is doable in the current build. It's more of just like an invoice kind of deal with static text that he can't change.

evaluated.name: Excel / SharePoint
evaluated.what_they_liked: Flexible enough to model any pricing scenario. Can maintain internal and external versions. Supports the branded proposal format they need.
evaluated.why_its_failing: Completely manual. Requires operations involvement on every quote. No guardrails or automated approval workflows. Rate year confusion. Cannot scale with growing sales team.
evaluated.key_quote: We're just trying to figure out how to get to market quicker and how to keep a data warehouse of kind of what we're doing.

current_workaround.description: Excel-based price book in SharePoint with branded Word/PDF templates. Operations lead builds quotes manually, cloning existing templates for common scenarios. CFO reviews every quote. Three quote types (admin, network, marketplace) with per-partner pricing tabs.
current_workaround.adequacy: Functional but does not scale. Operations lead is the bottleneck. Process breaks on novel deal structures. No data warehouse of quoting activity.

quotes.quote: Our desire is more of building this out so that we can feel confident that we don't have to do the QA anymore.
quotes.usage_notes: Core buying motivation. Directly maps to RevOps pain of being stuck in operational work instead of building scalable processes.

quotes.quote: I need to get out of doing these quotes with our CFO.
quotes.usage_notes: Perfect for content targeting operations and finance personas. Shows the dual bottleneck (ops + finance) on every quote.

quotes.quote: What if I accidentally do like Cigna's rate for 26, but it's a 27 quote? So that, it was just the way it was configured. So there's so many line items that can probably be eliminated.
quotes.usage_notes: Concrete error scenario. Strong for content about pricing accuracy and the hidden risks of spreadsheet-based price books.

quotes.quote: We're just trying to figure out how to get to market quicker and how to keep a data warehouse of kind of what we're doing.
quotes.usage_notes: VP of Sales perspective. Maps directly to quote velocity and visibility pain points. Two goals in one sentence.

quotes.quote: In the current configuration, cannot do this. David cannot edit this. None of this is doable in the current build. It's more of just like an invoice kind of deal with static text that he can't change.
quotes.usage_notes: Direct competitive evidence against HubSpot native CPQ. Shows the specific gap between what they need and what native quoting provides.

quotes.quote: I finally found in almost a year of doing this, David, the first scenario that we don't have a template that I can do it easily.
quotes.usage_notes: Shows how template-based quoting breaks on novel scenarios. Good for content about configuration flexibility vs. rigid templates.

quotes.quote: If you threw it into ChatGPT and said, 'Hey, what are the infinite outcomes for how many price books I could create here?' It'd be probably hundreds.
quotes.usage_notes: Illustrates the scale of their pricing complexity problem. Good for content about why simple price book approaches fail for multi-partner businesses.

quotes.quote: David can do up to a 10% discount, and then beyond that they gotta go to approval.
quotes.usage_notes: Shows they have clear discount rules but no automated way to enforce them. Maps to Finance/Controller persona pain.

quotes.quote: We typically just reduce ours. Unless it's a huge opportunity, then we will go to the partner and say, 'Hey, 21,000 lives. Yeah, they'll drop their fees.'
quotes.usage_notes: Reveals margin management behavior. Discounts come from Reflect Health's margin first, partner concessions only for large deals. Relevant for finance persona content.

quotes.quote: We've gotten a situation where they sign, they haven't signed those in the past, and then we end up... basically it's binding so that if we start work in lieu of a contract, they have to pay us something.
quotes.usage_notes: Shows need for e-signature and letter of intent as part of the proposal. Connects to proposal document completeness.

value_language: don't have to do the QA anymore
data warehouse of what we're doing

deviant_terms.meaning: Per Employee Per Month — their primary pricing unit for administrative services. Industry-specific term used repeatedly.
Reflect Health acting as a TPA serving other TPAs (a TPA for TPAs). Describes their unique market position of white-labeling services to other administrators.
deviant_terms.frequency: used once but represents a key business model nuance

deviant_terms.meaning: A pre-built, standardized bundle of products and pricing for a specific partner/scenario. Equivalent to a product bundle template.
Configured sets of services and partner products assembled for a specific customer scenario. Used interchangeably with bundles.
The full revenue potential of a customer including products not yet quoted. Used in context of expanding what they track on quotes.
Their service model when selling claims and customer service platform access to other TPAs.

keyword_candidates.source: direct pain — native HubSpot quoting produces invoice-style output, cannot include cover letters, editable text, or branded proposal sections

keyword_candidates.source: industry-specific — prospect is a healthcare TPA with unique pricing models (PEPM, percentage of savings, partner splits)
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why healthcare TPAs need CPQ: managing partner networks, PEPM pricing, and multi-tier margins in one system

keyword_candidates.source: stated rule — sales VP can discount up to 10%, beyond that requires finance approval. No automated enforcement today.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Setting discount guardrails in HubSpot: how to give sales reps pricing flexibility without losing margin control

keyword_candidates.source: direct competitive gap — prospect hit the wall with native HubSpot quoting on pricing flexibility, document customization, and bundling
keyword_candidates.content_angle: The 5 HubSpot native quoting limitations that force mid-market teams to look elsewhere (and what to do about them)

keyword_candidates.source: current state — entire quoting process runs on Excel in SharePoint. Looking to move into HubSpot-native CPQ.
